data.title: Market Sentiment Shifts As U.S. And Russia Navigate Ukraine Crisis
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In recent weeks, the market has been dominated by geopolitical issues, artificial intelligence and tariffs, but now a very important topic has returned to the negotiating table: the war in Ukraine.

In this article, I intend to inform the reader about the current situation. Additionally, I will answer the following questions: What are the impacts of resolving the conflict without consulting the allies? How is the market behaving? How should investors behave?

What Is Happening?

An emergency meeting of European leaders regarding the war in Ukraine took place on Monday. The leaders will discuss talks between the US and Russia to end the war, which will be 3 years old on February 24th. The meeting taking place against a backdrop of discontent with the Trump administration's decisions to care little about the presence of allies at the table.

What Is The European Fear?

In addition to their dissatisfaction with not having an active role in the negotiations, European military experts say that excessive concessions to Russia could put the continent's security at risk.

US Secretary of State Marco Rubio sought to calm the mood last Sunday (16) by stating that Europe would be part of the negotiations to end the war. In my view, it is clear that a mismatch between the US and its allies could weaken their alliances in future discussions.

It is worth remembering that the US has a direct influence on investments in several countries, and disruptions can impact the prices of ETFs that replicate assets from these geographies like iShares MSCI United Kingdom ETF (EWU), iShares MSCI Netherlands ETF (EWN), iShares MSCI Ireland ETF (EIRL) among others.

It is worth remembering that we have complex situations between China and Taiwan, and the Middle East, among others. But after all, what is the Trump administration's interest in ending the war?

Interests In Ending The War

In addition to the quest for peace, the Trump administration has reportedly proposed that the U.S. take a 50% stake in Ukraine's rare earth minerals. This would be a way to compensate the US for military aid provided to Ukraine.

These rare earths contain lanthanum, cerium, neodymium, erbium, and yttrium among others. But why is this important to the US? It would reduce the gap between the US and Russia in terms of rare earths and have a strong long-term impact on the economy and investment.

However, the Ukrainian President rejected the deal and stated that any exploration of minerals by the US would be linked to security guarantees.

Another point is that the end of the war could reduce the price of oil, which would contribute to reducing global inflation. Additionally, it could reverse the price trend seen in January 2024 for crude oil (CL1:COM) and would allow central banks to further reduce interest rates to stimulate economies.

After this contextualization, the proximity of discussions between the Trump administration and Russia has had an impact on the market. We'll talk more about this in the next chapter.

The Market Is Anticipating A Resolution Of The Conflict

Signaling from the Trump administration has led the betting market to predict a 50% chance of a ceasefire this year and a 34% chance of it occurring in the first 90 days of Trump's term.

Since then, investors have been confident about stocks of companies that stand to benefit from Ukraine’s reconstruction such as Schneider Electric S.E. (OTCPK:SBGSF), Stora Enso Oyj (OTCQX:SEOAY) and others listed by UBS analysts. The full index is below.

I need to say that I do not consider investing in companies based on news speculation. Basically, news-driven price volatility triggers human behaviors such as herd mentality, or in this case, the fear of missing out.

This is harmful since all of these risks can be partially mitigated with a well-balanced portfolio. It is even more advisable for investors to exercise caution when analyzing current market valuation.

Valuation

Although the resolution of the conflict in Ukraine reduces the risk that investors see in the macroeconomic and geopolitical scenario, I do not feel comfortable investing in the main American indexes at current prices, as we can see below.

SPY, which replicates the S&P 500 index in an ETF, has the highest P/E in the last 3 years. The asset traded at its highest at 23x earnings and at its lowest at 15x earnings, and it spent most of its time trading close to 20x earnings.

If we assume that it returns to trading at 20x earnings, there is a downside of 13%, and this corroborates my recommendation to hold. For the investor who believes that the current moment demands a strategically chosen portfolio, I offer my recommended portfolio.

Potential Threats To The Bullish Thesis

There are risks to the thesis. If the conflict is resolved, there is a good chance that European assets (which remain quite undervalued) will appreciate, which could create a rotation and harm portfolios exposed only to American assets.

Likewise, gold prices could fall, as there is a reduction in geopolitical risk. Commodities could be repriced by investors, given the relevance of Russia and Ukraine to the world.

Last but not least, Seeking Alpha's powerful Quant tools recommend that investors buy the SPY ETF, which goes against my skeptical thesis and option for a strategically chosen portfolio.

The Bottom Line

An important conflict in the geopolitical environment may be close to being resolved: the war in Ukraine. But will its resolution alone be enough to restore some global harmony? Apparently not.

Europeans and Ukrainians are dissatisfied with the Trump administration's conduct of negotiations. Meanwhile, the market prices a 34% chance of the conflict being resolved in the first 90 days of Trump's term.

This could cause a major repricing of assets, from European stocks to commodities. But it is important for investors to remember that it is not interesting to invest based on news, but rather to have a balanced portfolio.

Based on this analysis, I recommend holding assets that replicate the main American indices. Valuation does not match the geopolitical risks and implications that an unresolved conflict could have. In my view, investors need to choose their portfolio strategically.

data.title: The Stocks And ETFs Driving Trump's Tariff Target Markets
data.description:
Conversations about Trump’s tariffs, despite two of them being placed on hold for now, continue to dominate market discussions. Although it could be different this time, historically these announcements cause volatility in the short run. But by about the time they become reality, if not before, their effects are absorbed by the market with barely a hiccup to most stocks. Christina Hooper, Invesco Chief Global Market Strategist, wrote today that “I’m cautiously optimistic that while we may see lots of drama, we may not see a meaningful long-term market impact.” This is based partially upon the overall effect of the 2018 China tariffs and partially because the truth espoused by John Maynard Keynes that, in the short-term, the market is a voting machine. In the long-term, it is a weighing machine as its focus eventually returns to earnings.

The three countries in questions are Canada, China, and Mexico. This blog dives into several major companies domiciled in one of those three countries. We’ll start with the seven companies currently rated Buy or Strong Buy in Canada.

First Quantum Minerals Ltd., (OTCPK:FQVLF) together with its subsidiaries, engages in the exploration, development, and production of mineral properties. Germane minerals include: copper, nickel, pyrite, silver, gold, and zinc. Its strong compound earnings growth rate sets it somewhat apart from its rivals in this sector. The company is based in Vancouver, Canada.

Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce (CM), a diversified financial institution, provides various financial products and services to personal, business, public sector, and institutional clients in Canada, the United States, and internationally. The company operates through Canadian Personal and Business Banking; Canadian Commercial Banking and Wealth Management; U.S. Commercial Banking and Wealth Management; Capital Markets and Direct Financial Services; and Corporate and Other segments. Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce was founded in 1867 and is headquartered in Toronto, Canada.

Shopify Inc. (SHOP), a commerce company, provides a commerce platform and services in Canada, the United States, Europe, the Middle East, Africa, the Asia Pacific, Australia, China, and Latin America. The company’s platform enables merchants to display, manage, market, and sell their products through various sales channels that include web and mobile storefronts, physical retail locations, pop-up shops, social media storefronts, native mobile apps, buy buttons, and marketplaces. It also sells custom themes and apps, registration of domain names and merchant solutions, which include accepting payments, shipping, and securing working capital. The company was formerly known as Jaded Pixel Technologies Inc. and changed its name to Shopify Inc. in November 2011. Shopify Inc.(orig. Jaded Pixel Tech.) was incorporated in 2004 and is headquartered in Ottawa, Canada.

Brookfield Corp. (BN) is an alternative asset manager and REIT/Real Estate Investment Manager firm focused on real estate, renewable power, infrastructure and venture capital and private equity assets. It manages a range of public and private investment products and services for institutional and retail clients. It typically makes investments in sizable, premier assets across geographies and asset classes. It invests both its own capital as well as capital from other investors. Within private equity and venture capital, it focuses on acquisition, early ventures, control buyouts and financially distressed buyouts and corporate carve-outs, recapitalizations, convertible, senior and mezzanine financings, operational and capital structure restructuring, strategic re-direction, turnaround, and under-performing midmarket companies. BN has a strong and consistent historical record of profitability. Brookfield Corporation was founded in 1997 and based in Toronto, Canada with additional offices across Northern America; South America; Europe; Middle East and Asia.

Manulife Financial Corporation (MFC), together with its subsidiaries, provides financial products and services in the United States, Canada, Asia, and internationally. The company operates through Wealth and Asset Management Businesses; Insurance and Annuity Products; and Corporate and Other segments. The company was incorporated in 1887 and is headquartered in Toronto, Canada.

Royal Bank of Canada (RY) operates as a diversified financial service company worldwide. Its major operating segments are: Personal & Commercial Banking; Wealth Management; Financial Institutions; Insurance; and Capital Markets. The company was founded in 1864 and is headquartered in Toronto, Canada.

GFL Environmental Inc. (GFL)offers non-hazardous solid waste management and environmental services in Canada and the United States. The company was incorporated in 2007 and is headquartered in Vaughan, Canada.

Here are the ratings, average trading volume (important for foreign stocks trading on US exchanges) and the current market price vs. fair value.

Ticker Company Name VE Rating Avg Trading

Volume ($)

 Market Price Fair Value Industry Name
FQVLF) First Quantum 5 223,192 12.67 12.19 MINING-NON FERROUS
CM Canadian Imperial Bank 4 975,109 63.02 48.31 BANKS-FOREIGN
SHOP Shopify Inc. 4 7,979,262 116.8 99.94 INTERNET SERVICES
BN Brookfield Corporation 4 2,070,493 61.2 45.51 REAL ESTATE OPERATIONS
MFC Manulife Financial 4 2,030,410 29.91 23.08 INSURANCE-LIFE
RY Royal Bank of Canada 4 1,084,039 121.93 99.77 BANKS-FOREIGN
GFL GFL Environmental 4 1,434,969 43.13 32.30 WASTE REMOVAL SVCS

FQVLF is the only top-ranked 5 (Strong Buy) stock according to ValuEngine’s predictive forecast model. The other six stocks are rated 4 (Buy). Even though all seven stocks are rated buys on a relative basis, our valuation model has them all trading above fair value. This would be indicative of a market that is largely overpriced at current levels. That said, these stocks still are forecast to outperform their market peers.

In terms of the Trump tariffs, firms that ship a lot of physical exports to the USA would be the most vulnerable to getting disproportionately savaged in profit margins and sales. Of this group, only top-ranked First Quantum is a heavy exporter. The other six stocks would get some potential peripheral pain if and when key customers have their sales and earnings impacted negatively by the tariffs. Typically, that would take longer to digest and by that time, tariffs might be abated or waived altogether.

Equally important in examining firms that could be hurt by Trump tariffs is to look at the stocks forecast to underperform in 2025. In Canada, these include:

Celestica Inc. (CLS) provides supply chain solutions in North America, Europe, and Asia. It operates through two segments: Advanced Technology Solutions, and Connectivity & Cloud Solutions. The company offers a range of manufacturing and related supply chain services. It also provides hardware platform solutions and management of programs including design, supply chain and manufacturing. Celestica Inc. was incorporated in 1994 and is headquartered in Toronto, Canada.

Lululemon Athletica Inc., (LULU) together with its subsidiaries, designs, distributes, and retails athletic apparel, footwear, and accessories under the lululemon brand for women and men. It offers pants, shorts, tops, and jackets for a healthy lifestyle, such as yoga, running, training, and other activities. It also provides fitness-inspired accessories. The company sells its products through a chain of company-operated stores, outlets, interactive workout platforms, yoga and fitness studios, university campus retailers, and other partners, license and supply arrangements, as well as through mobile apps and lululemon e-commerce website. Lululemon Athletica Inc. was founded in 1998 and is based in Vancouver, Canada.

Canadian Natural Resources Ltd. (CNQ) acquires, explores for, develops, produces, markets, and sells crude oil, natural gas, and natural gas liquids (NGLs). The company offers light and medium crude oil, primary heavy crude oil, Pelican Lake heavy crude oil, bitumen (thermal oil), and synthetic crude oil (SCO). Canadian Natural Resources Limited was incorporated in 1973 and is headquartered in Calgary, Canada.

Canadian Pacific Kansas City Limited (CP), together with its subsidiaries, owns and operates a transcontinental freight railway in Canada, the United States, and Mexico. The company transports bulk commodities, merchandise freight, and intermodal traffic. It also provides rail and intermodal transportation services over a network of approximately 20,000 miles. Canadian Pacific Kansas City Limited was incorporated in 1881 and is headquartered in Calgary, Canada.

Teck Resources Limited (TECK) engages in exploring for, acquiring, developing, and producing natural resources in Asia, Europe, and North America. The company operates through Steelmaking Coal, Copper, Zinc, and Energy segments. Its principal products include copper, zinc, steelmaking coal, and blended bitumen. Formerly Teck Cominco, it was founded in 1913 and is headquartered in Vancouver, Canada.

Unlike the list of seven buy-rated companies, the five stocks rated 2 or below are major exporters to the USA. Our models don’t account for tariffs, so analysts may have already slashed earnings estimates due to potential protectionist policies by the Trump regime. At any rate, these stocks are seen as particularly vulnerable to underperformance in the next 12 months. Here is the data for these five stocks. Please note that all five are trading substantially above their fair value targets.

Ticker Company Name VE Rating Avg Trading Volume ($) Market Price Fair Value Industry Name
CLS Celestica Inc. 1 3030799 123.47 52.39 ELEC MANUF SVS (EMS)
LULU Lululemon 2 1930905 414.20 356.74 TEXTILE-APPAREL MFG
CNQ Canadian National Resources 2 4920368 30.35 23.03 OIL & GAS-CDN EXP & PROD
CP Canadian Pacific Kansas City 2 3243517 79.60 62.50 TRANSPORTATION-RAIL
TECK Teck Resources 2 2611762 40.88 23.87 MINING-MISC

Moving on to China, currently five of the US-listed stocks associated with China-domiciled companies are rated 5 (Strong Buy).

Ticker Company Name VE Rating Avg Trading Volume ($) Market Price Fair Value Industry Name
JD JD.com, Inc.-ADR 5 1084354900 40.72 39.95 INTERNET COMMERCE
BABA Alibaba Group 5 1.6459902E7 98.84 130.74 INTERNET COMMERCE
TCEHY Tencent Holding 5 2905954 53.02 49.24 INTERNET SERVICES
YUMC Yum China Holdings 5 2271291 46.25 47.12 RETAIL-FOOD & RESTAURANTS
ZTO ZTO Express 5 2985079 18.66 23.16 TRANSPORTATION-SVCS

Three of them are internet-based companies. JD is a massive e-retailer that has branched into other areas such as health care; it has been referred to as the Amazon of China. Alibaba Group Holding Limited, through its subsidiaries, provides technology infrastructure and marketing reach to help merchants, brands, retailers, and other businesses to engage with their users and customers in the People’s Republic of China and internationally. Tencent Holdings (OTCPK:TCEHY) offers value-added services (VAS), online advertising, fintech, and business services in the People’s Republic of China and internationally. The company’s consumer business provides communication and services, such as instant messaging and social network, fintech support, and digital content including online games, etc.

The other two 5-rated Chinese companies are Yum China (YUMC) and ZTO Express (ZTO). Yum China owns, operates, and franchises restaurants in the People’s Republic of China. The company operates through KFC, Pizza Hut, Taco Bell and is similar to the US version of Yum Brands. ZTO Express provides express delivery and other value-added logistics services. There is just one stock trading a bit below its fair value, Yum China. In contrast to these highly rated stocks, none of the Chinese ADRs above the $10 billion market cap that we cover are rated below 3 (Hold). In terms of the potential effects of the tariffs, YUMC and ZTO do not operate outside China so it seems probable that any effects on their revenues or profits would be peripheral. The three internet companies derive most of their revenues from within China, not from exports, so they also seem unlikely to have major impacts to their profits from the tariffs. As far as Mexico is concerned, none of the market-cap-qualifying ADRs covered by ValuEngine are rated anything but a 3 (Hold).

From stocks, we move on to the major US-listed ETFs for Canada, China and Mexico. Altogether, there are 34 ETFs that cover China. There are three ETFs apiece that cover Canada and Mexico. The following table lists the top 10 non-leverages funds in descending order of assets under management.

# Sym Name Total Asset($Mil.) Avg. Daily $ Volume (,000s) Price 1 Month Return 1 Year Return 5 Year Return Expense Ratio
1 (BBCA) JPMorgan BetaBuilders Canada ETF 7,885 268 $74.08 5.92% 18.07% 9.66% 0.19%
2 (FXI) iShares China Large-Cap ETF 7,718 42,667 $35.04 21.71% 56.74% -1.52% 0.74%
3 (KWEB) KraneShares CSI China Internet ETF 7,416 19,391 $35.65 29.78% 46.37% -5.18% 0.70%
4 (MCHI) iShares MSCI China ETF 5,549 2,963 $53.46 21.86% 43.27% -1.89% 0.59%
5 (EWC) iShares MSCI Canada ETF 2,775 2,383 $42.17 5.85% 17.52% 9.04% 0.50%
6 (ASHR) X-trackers Harvest CSI 300 China A-Shares ETF 2,276 8,828 $27.18 8.59% 18.76% 0.61% 0.65%
7 (EWW) iShares MSCI Mexico ETF 1,481 2,324 $52.67 10.98% -18.93% 4.93% 0.50%
8 (CQQQ) Invesco China Technology ETF 815 288 $47.32 28.59% 54.34% -3.32% 0.65%
9 (GXC) SPDR S&P China ETF 423 36 $84.82 19.22% 38.40% -1.63% 0.59%
10 (FLCA) Franklin FTSE Canada ETF 416 32 $38.42 5.81% 18.60% 9.78% 0.09%

This list is composed of six Chinese, three Canadian, and one Mexican equity ETF. Given the number of ETFs in total and the relative sizes of the countries and their stock markets, it is not surprising that ETF’s covering China dominate this list. What was surprising to me was that the top ETF in terms of assets under management represented Canada and not China.

Also surprising is that the ETF in question, JPMorgan BetaBuilders Canada (BBCA), started in August of 2018 making it the youngest ETF on the list. The implication is that it has had the shortest amount of time to gather assets. Yet it leads this top 10 list with nearly eight billion dollars under management. Also interesting is the fact that while BBCA leads the list in AUM, it is only eighth highest on the list in daily dollar trading volume. iShares China Large Cap ETF (FXI) is second on the list in assets under management and tops in trading volume. FXI trades an average daily dollar volume of more than 150 times that of asset-leader BBCA. Is this because traders can make money trading China, but investors prefer to invest in Canada? That certainly isn’t the entire story. Expense ratios may also play a factor. BBCA charges an expense ratio of 0.19% as compared with a whopping 0.74% for FXI. The expense ratio means virtually nothing to traders with a holding period of a week or less. But more than half a percent per year amounts to a sizable amount of money for long-term buy-and-hold investors. It could also be that JP Morgan excels at raising investment assets from institutions but does not focus on the needs of trading desks as much.

FXI is one of four ETFs that charge expense ratios of 65 basis points or higher. All four of these ETFs focus on a particular area of the Chinese stock market rather than one of the generic country indexes. The rationale may be that such “value-added” indexes require more research materials and time. FXI has the best one-year performance of 56% and the China ETFs dominate the top performers for the one-month and one-year periods. The story gets reversed when the time frame is stretched to five years. Franklin FTSE Canada ETF (FCLA) delivered the best return of 9.8%. Its holders also profited from it having the lowest expense ratio of 0.09% (9 basis points). In fact, all three Canada ETFs on the list handily outperformed the China ETFs over the 5-year time frame with returns between 9.1% and 9.8%. Mexico with a 4.9% 5 year return finished fourth overall behind the three Canadian ETFs. The only China ETF to exhibit a positive return during the 5-year period was X-Trackers Harvest CSI 300 China A-Shares (ASHR) with 0.6%.

The questions about what effect Trump’s tariffs will have on US investors with holdings in these three markets will remain unanswered for quite some time. In the long-term scheme of things the fundamentals of the companies behind these ADRs and stocks are likely to be more deterministic than any geopolitical factors.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Wayfair: Strong Execution In A Challenging Environment
data.description:
Some of the businesses that experienced the biggest booms during the pandemic still haven't recovered to those heights: and chief among those is Wayfair (NYSE:W), the online furniture company. During COVID, the plethora of people moving to the suburbs created unprecedented demand for furniture, to a degree that Wayfair experienced significant stock-outs and had to invest deeply into its logistics network to avoid constraining demand.

Today, however, high mortgage rates and low housing supply have dented the number of people changing homes, which in turn has impacted the demand for furniture. Wayfair's recent Q4 earnings print shows a category that is struggling to return to growth: but at the same time, the company is making meaningful operational tweaks to prepare itself for success when the category normalizes. The stock wavered between gains and losses after the earnings print; but over the past year, shares of Wayfair are down more than 10%.

I last wrote a neutral opinion on Wayfair in November, back when the stock was trading at similar ~$44 levels. Since then, Wayfair's Q4 earnings print has moved me in a slightly more positive direction on the stock. I appreciate the company's revenue trends improving back to barely flat (as opposed to several quarters of decline in FY24), while at the same time improving its structural profitability. The company is also actively chasing supply in new categories that are seeing the highest growth (such as outdoor structures and spas, as well as recreational products) in order to try to reinvigorate marketplace sales. I see the company as one that will be able to manage the downturn and emerge stronger: but at the same time, challenging conditions will persist, and we likely won't see a resurgence in furniture growth until the housing market gets back on track. With this in mind, I'm reiterating my neutral opinion on Wayfair.

On the bright side for Wayfair: it's important to recognize all the steps that Wayfair has taken to improve its structural profitability. One major example of this is the company's CastleGate logistics program, increasing the density of its shipping networks in order to more efficiently route products to buyers. These actions have taken Wayfair to a 3.8% adjusted EBITDA margin in FY24.

Its goal, as shown in the chart above, is actually to exceed 10%+ margins. The company is targeting economies of scale on advertising, further efficiencies on logistics, as well as trimming on opex in order to get there.

The chart below showcases how Wayfair has already made tremendous progress on bringing down corporate overhead. On a TTM basis, annual overhead (excluding stock-based comp) hit $1.6 billion, which is a ~24% reduction from pandemic-era peaks at $2.1 billion.

Of course, the path forward for Wayfair isn't a clean one. The company is battling an uphill battle against a tough real estate market plus a tight macro environment, in which many consumers are trimming their budgets. Furniture is a long-lasting product: more people may decide to hang onto their furnishings for longer, lengthening replacement cycles.

All in all, the bull-versus-bear case for Wayfair is rather balanced. Keep watching this stock closely, but don't rush in to buy.

Q4 download

Let's now go through Wayfair's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Wayfair's revenue was flat at 0.2% y/y growth to $3.12 billion, coming in ahead of Wall Street's expectations of $3.07 billion (-1.3% y/y) by a 150bps margin. Importantly as well, the company's growth trends returned to flat after three straight quarters of decline in the -2% range.

The company notes that after several quarters of softer demand, the company's "Wayborhood" brand campaigns struck a positive note during the holiday season, which the company said translated to double-digit growth in app installs. This is a hopeful indicator: furniture is a long-tail, heavily researched decision, and these new app installs may help kick off more robust revenue growth in the quarters to come. Per CEO Niraj Shah's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

In the fourth quarter, we launched our first holiday installment of the Wayborhood and continue to see powerful positive impacts across awareness, brand linkage and recall rates. This is resonating across the business in multiple ways. We saw healthy double-digit growth and app installs during the fourth quarter, and we saw new customer order growth in the U.S. outpace repeat for the first time since 2020. Pinterest continues to be one of our most powerful channels to speak with home shoppers. And we saw very robust double-digit growth and visits during the fourth quarter.

We're very excited about our plans ahead for 2025. We've got new campaigns, new influencer partnerships, and continue to test it in newer channels for us, such as YouTube and AppLovin. This testing stage has been a core part of our expertise in digital marketing for years. We take a thoughtful and considered approach as we look to develop new channels, testing customer response aggressively, and only choosing to scale where we see clear evidence of payback. These investments are table setting for substantial returns, not just in the quarters ahead, but also for years down the road."

On the profitability front: gross margins fell slightly by 10bps to 30.2% in Q4, down from 30.3% in the year-ago quarter. From a full-year perspective, gross margin was also 30.2%, 40bps weaker y/y. The company suffered from deleveraging on declining sales volumes. Still, we do note that the company's ability to maintain a ~30% gross margin is impressive as it's higher than the ~28% that it notched during the pandemic years, when Wayfair's revenue actually peaked.

Meanwhile, the company's opex reductions have been able to offset the gross margin losses, with adjusted EBITDA growing 4% y/y to $96 million - or a 3.1% margin, 10bps better y/y.

Key takeaways

In my view, Wayfair is a company in the midst of a multi-year transition. It's laying the groundwork in logistics and brand campaigning to succeed when the furniture market (and to a large extent, the real estate industry) turn around. Still, we note that with Wayfair trading at ~14x forward adjusted EBITDA, it's not exactly a value stock despite the difficulty of its position right now.

In my view, Wayfair's path to recovery is likely still beyond 2025, so I'd prefer to stay on the sidelines for now.

data.title: Compass: Hold Rated As I Am Still Very Worried About The Risks
data.description:
Investment action

I recommended a hold rating for Compass (NYSE:COMP) when I wrote about it last September, as I expected an uncertain period ahead for COMP because of the various business risks I have identified. Based on my current outlook and analysis, I recommend a hold rating. While I like the stronger-than-expected execution, I am still very worried about the poor housing demand situation in the US, plagued by stubbornly high mortgage rates, and also the potential regulatory risks tied to COMP’s private inventory model. Given these factors, I believe maintaining a neutral stance is the most prudent approach at this time.

Review

COMP reported 4Q24 revenue of ~$1.4 billion, reflecting 25.9% y/y growth. The strong top-line performance was driven by a 24.1% y/y increase in transactions, significantly outpacing the broader US market’s 6.8% y/y growth. Organic transaction growth came in at 15.5% y/y, indicating that growth was not entirely acquisition driven. COMP also continues to win market share, with its current share increased to 5.06%, up 65 basis points y/y and 26 basis points sequentially. In terms of profitability, COMP reported adj EBITDA of $17 million, which was a sharp improvement from the -$23.7 million seen in 4Q23. For 1Q25, management guided revenue to $1.35 billion to $1.475 billion, and adj EBITDA in the range from $11 million to $25 million.

It was very pleasing to hear that COMP continues to gain market share. As of 4Q24, COMP has posted four consecutive quarters of y/y market share expansion, and I think a key reason is because COMP has managed to continue signing new principal agents, which has increased to 17,752, up 21% y/y growth, with retention remaining strong at 96.9%. I see this as a virtuous cycle, wherein COMP gains more market share, giving it more financial capacity to reinvest in the business (in tech or even potentially better commission rates) which helps it to attract more agents, which leads to more capacity to capture market share. Management expects to continue growing its agent base by 600-700 net additions per quarter, and if they are able to achieve this, I believe COMP is well on track to reach 30% market share in its top 30 markets by 2026 (management’s goal as noted in the 3Q24 earnings call). Winning market share (and share of principal agents) also means that competitors competitive positions weaken, as this is a zero-sum game—agents that work under COMP would mean that all potential leads belong to COMP.

COMP's private inventory strategy—where agents list homes as Private Exclusives or Coming Soon before they appear on the MLS (multiple listing service)—is also becoming a key differentiator. Currently, 35% of all COMP listings are either Private Exclusives or Coming Soon, and 55% of all new February listings had started as private listings. The impact of this strategy is a lot better than I had imagined. According to management, early results suggest that homes marketed under this model are receiving accepted offers 20% faster on average and experiencing fewer price reductions. Internal data also indicates that homes pre-marketed as Private Exclusives closed at an average premium of 2.9% compared to similar properties that went directly to the MLS. What I can infer is that this model has managed to create a “sense of urgency” for buyers. From the buyer’s perspective, if they really like the home, they have to get it now; if not, there could be more competitive buyers when it's being shifted to MLS. I still think we need to see a couple more quarters of performance to determine if this strategy works as well as it seems now. But if COMP can continue scaling this strategy, it could help heavily strengthen agent loyalty (since they earn more) and drive higher revenue growth.

Housing Market Weakness and Affordability Challenges

Despite COMP's strong execution, I am still very wary about the US macroeconomic conditions. In particular, I think the weak housing market will weigh on COMP’s ability to grow. Mortgage rates continue to stay at a stubborn level of ~7%, and no matter how good COMP’s strategy may be, it does not improve housing affordability, and a weak housing market means fewer transaction volumes. The Fed's messaging has also become increasingly conservative relative to last year. Just yesterday, the Fed meeting minutes showed that the Fed is looking for more progress on inflation coming down before cutting rates. We know from the latest inflation data that CPI has actually gone up. It seems to me that rates are unlikely to see major cuts this year, and that is not great for the mortgage rates outlook. The January 2025 home sales data, per the National Association of Realtors, also suggest a y/y decline, suggesting that housing activity remains sluggish. If mortgage rates stay elevated longer than expected, I believe it will dampen COMP's ability to sustain its current growth trajectory.

Regulatory Uncertainty Around Private Inventory Model

Another concern I have is regarding the potential for increased regulatory scrutiny of COMP’s Private Exclusive and Coming Soon strategy. The National Association of Realtors’ Clear Cooperation Rule mandates that most listings must appear on the MLS within one business day of being publicly marketed. While COMP has argued that enforcement has been inconsistent, a crackdown could force COMP to shift its strategy. Currently, management believes the rule is no longer enforced in nearly half of its markets, but this does not mean that regulatory will not come back and enforce it. If they do, it could limit the potential upside from this private inventory strategy.

Valuation

The market is seemingly focused on the potential upside and giving credit to COMP’s execution abilities. COMP’s forward EBITDA multiple has shot to its one-year high (19x), with the share price touching ~$10, a level it has not seen since late 2021. I am still not fully convinced that COMP's growth trajectory is clear. I think investors should not simply shake off the risks that I have mentioned above, especially the one about mortgage rates pressuring housing demand. If you recall what happened in September24, the COMP multiple also went up to ~18x, but valuation fell all the way back to 11x over the next three months. If the housing market demand situation gets worse, my fear is that this will repeat again as market participants become conservative on their growth expectations.

Final thoughts

My recommendation remains a hold. COMP has demonstrated impressive execution, with continued market share expansion, strong agent retention, and early success with its private inventory strategy. However, I still think the poor housing demand environment will weigh on COMP’s performance. The high mortgage rates and less likelihood of rate cuts by the Fed are concerning. Additionally, regulatory uncertainty around COMP’s private listings model could disrupt one of COMP’s key differentiators.

data.title: SentinelOne: Why I Am Buying More Now
data.description:
SentinelOne, Inc. (NYSE:S) is one of the most unique companies in the AI software space. What makes SentinelOne special is that it may be one of the purest AI plays in the lucrative global cybersecurity market. Another factor that makes SentinelOne very attractive is that it is an "under-the-radar" stock right now, and it has a relatively cheap valuation.

Moreover, SentinelOne will report earnings soon, on March 12th, and there is a high probability that SentinelOne could report and guide better than expected, in my view. Another element to consider is that SentinelOne recently caught a downgrade from UBS, which has caused its stock to slide, creating a compelling buying opportunity around the crucial $23-22 support area.

Technically: A Very Bullish Set Up

SentinelOne's stock has been beaten down recently, and it shed about 15% in 3 days following a UBS downgrade. We also witnessed a bullish reversal, as the stock bounced off the crucial $22 support. We could see a follow-through of the bullish reversal, and SentinelOne may move above $25 resistance to $30, and potentially higher, to about $35 by year-end. This increase would equate to an upside of roughly 60%, making SentinelOne a highly compelling buying opportunity in my view.

The Timely UBS Downgrade

While I and others may be bullish on SentinelOne, some aren't, and this is what makes a market. Roger Boyd from UBS recently slapped a downgrade on SentinelOne, citing some slower than expected growth metrics, the company's decision to reinvest in its business, and other factors. He decreased the price target from $30 to $25 on SentinelOne's stock, which may be the primary reason we saw the slide from $25 to $22 in recent sessions.

SentinelOne's AI Advantage

However, despite this negative note, SentinelOne's average price target remains around $30, representing the potential for about a 30% increase, in a base case outcome. Moreover, SentinelOne remains the AI leader in the cybersecurity space. Singularity is a top-rated cybersecurity platform, that enables unfettered visibility, industry-leading detection, and autonomous response.

SentinelOne may have the top AI-powered cybersecurity platform, which may perform the best relative to AI and other threats. This dynamic makes SentinelOne a top pick in the cybersecurity space, and we could see better than anticipated AI-powered efficiency and profitability ahead.

SentinelOne May Be Undervalued

It's crucial to recognize that SentinelOne is a relatively new company, founded just 12 years ago, and it went public around the height of the bull market in 2021, right before the savage tech bear market arrived.

SentinelOne traded up to a high of about $80 after it IPOed. In November 2021, its market cap eclipsed $20 billion. Given that SentinelOne had about $200 million in sales in 2021, during its peak, it traded at about 100 sales.

However, like many tech stocks, SentinelOne went through a tumultuous bear market, with its share price finally bottoming around the $13-15 range, and the stock hasn't climbed much from this ultra-low range yet.

SentinelOne's Valuation Looks Much Different Now

First, I want to point out that SentinelOne is going through a critical transformation stage as it prepares to transition to a sustainable, profitable company. SentinelOne could go from almost no or very little in EPS to roughly $0.20 cents in fiscal 2026, and it could double the EPS to $0.40 or more in fiscal 2027.

However, SentinelOne is only transitioning to just become slightly profitable. Therefore, it's challenging to use its potential P/E ratio to derive a valuation. Yet, we can look at sales figures. SentinelOne should have about $1-1.1B in sales in fiscal 2026.

Surprising for its growth prospects, SentinelOne's market cap is only about $7.8 billion. This dynamic illustrates that SentinelOne trades at only around 7 times sales. Please consider that this valuation is for a high-quality, high growth, AI-leading cybersecurity company in the lucrative AI software space.

SentinelOne Has Substantial Growth Potential

Due to its emphasis on growth and considerable expansion potential, SentinelOne could continue posting 25-35% YoY sales growth for several years as we advance.

With this type of growth potential, it is atypical to see this high quality/high potential stock trading at only 7-8 times forward sales, especially in this market. This phenomenon suggests that the market may be missing the bigger image regarding S, and is unjustly suppressing its stock price here.

Here is how SentinelOne stacks up against other cybersecurity stocks:

  • CrowdStrike Holdings, Inc. (CRWD) trades at about 24 times forward sales estimates, making it roughly three times as expensive as SentinelOne here. It's also expected to post lower sales growth than SentinelOne.
  • Palo Alto Networks, Inc. (PANW) trades at about 14 times forward sales estimates, making it about two times as expensive as SentinelOne here. Moreover, Palo Alto will likely only deliver about 15% sales growth in future years.
  • Zscaler, Inc. (ZS) trades at about 11 times forward sales, making it about 50% more expensive than SentinelOne here, and it's also expected to post slower sales growth than SentinelOne.

The Takeaway

We see a divergence here, as the highest growth potential stock also trades at the cheapest multiple. Therefore, SentinelOne appears undervalued here. A more fairer P/S ratio that would be more in line with the industry standards here could push SentinelOne's P/S valuation to about 15, equating to a stock price of about $50 in approximately one year from now.

Where SentinelOne's stock could be in the future

Year (fiscal) 2026 2027 2028 2029 2030
Sales (in billions) $1.08 $1.39 $1.78 $2.28 $2.89
Sales growth 32% 29% 29% 28% 27%
EPS $0.28 $0.55 $0.91 $1.23 $1.57
EPS growth 833% 96% 65% 35% 28%
Forward P/E ratio

65

 63 60 58 55
Stock price $36 $57 $75 $92 $110

Source: The Financial Prophet

Due to its robust sales growth and profitability potential, SentinelOne may be capable of sustaining a relatively high P/E multiple as it becomes increasingly profitable in future years. Therefore, we could see considerable EPS increases enabling SentinelOne's stock to move significantly higher as we advance. This dynamic makes SentinelOne my top pick in the lucrative cybersecurity space for 2025 and 2026, with a $35-55 price target for the next 12-24 month period.

Risks to SentinelOne

SentinelOne faces risks despite my bullish assessment. The most considerable risk for SentinelOne may be the intense competition from CrowdStrike Holdings, Inc. (CRWD) and other top cybersecurity companies in this lucrative arena. SentinelOne also faces efficiency and profitability issues and must control costs carefully to keep up with EPS forecasts and continue expanding operations at a healthy pace. Investors should consider these and other risks before investing in SentinelOne's stock.

data.title: Sturm, Ruger & Company Vs. Smith & Wesson: Which American Gun Manufacturer Wins In A Shootout?
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Sturm, Ruger and Company (NYSE:RGR) (known as Ruger) and Smith & Wesson (NASDAQ:SWBI) are two American gun manufacturers with long successful histories and enthusiastic customers. Both companies have faced significant share price pullbacks recently, which has prompted me to analyze each company in an effort to determine which would make a better investment. I've ultimately ended up favoring Ruger over Smith & Wesson, although neither company is priced attractively enough yet to compensate for low growth prospects going forward.

Industry Overview

For a country so obsessed with guns, I was a bit surprised to find that America has just two publicly traded gun makers – Smith & Wesson and Ruger. While these two are the only investment options for those looking to capitalize on America’s enthusiastic gunslingers, they’re far from the only option down at your local gun shop. See the latest industry hierarchy in the chart below.

Ruger takes the top spot in volume with Smith & Wesson coming in third. Many names on this list, including Glock and SIG Sauer, are popular amongst handgun buyers but remain privately owned. While this article is focused on the two public companies on the list above, keep in mind that the industry is far from a duopoly.

Smith & Wesson was founded in 1852 by Horace Smith and Daniel B. Wesson in Springfield, Massachusetts. The firm has produced guns for the military going all the way back to the civil war and created cultural icons such as the .44 Magnum made famous by Clint Eastwood’s “Dirty Harry” in 1971. Tomkins PLC purchased Smith & Wesson in 1987 and entered into a controversial agreement with the Clinton administration to install internal locks on new firearms. Sales fell as a result of these measures and Tomkins eventually sold Smith & Wesson to Saf-T-Hammer Corporation in 2001. The combined company was rebranded back under the Smith & Wesson name and worked to regain the trust of the gun-buying public. More recently, Smith & Wesson in 2020 spun off its outdoor accessories business (American Outdoor Brands) and moved corporate headquarters from gun-weary Massachusetts to a more supportive environment in Tennessee. Today the company leverages its long history and reputation for reliability to support its brand power.

Sturm, Ruger & Co. was launched in a small shop in Southport, Ct in 1949 by William Ruger and Alexander Sturm. The new company’s first product, a .22 LR semi-automatic pistol inspired by the German Luger P08 and Japanese Nambu Type A pistols, became immensely popular due to its affordability, reliability and ease of use. The company has since expanded into the market for rifles and AR style weapons, with a proven track record of producing popular guns for a broad range of users.

Both companies focus on pistols, revolvers and rifles and have stellar reputations. My research covering industry publications, YouTube reviews and Reddit forums reveals two well-loved brands. For example, there are countless forum discussions where a poster is seeking advice on whether to purchase gun A from Ruger versus gun B from Smith & Wesson. The general consensus in most cases is that you can’t go wrong with either. If I were trying to tease out some difference between these company’s products based on online debates, Ruger may have a slightly better reputation for reliability while Smith & Wesson is perceived as a more fashionable and customizable choice. But again, the differences are very subtle, and I wouldn’t say either brand is clearly favored in the gun-owning community.

Balance Sheet Analysis

The firearms market can fluctuate wildly from year to year based on a range of factors including political developments, economic conditions and product innovation.

For that reason, companies in this industry are wise to maintain a conservative capital structure.

Measure

Smith & Wesson

Ruger

Long-Term Debt ($ millions)

$100

$0

Net Debt

$0

$0

Debt/Equity Ratio

0.59

0

As shown in the table above, Ruger carries no long-term debt. Smith & Wesson, on the other hand, carries $100 million on a 6.92% revolving credit line as of their most recent 10-Q. Though their current assets ($306 million) are greater than total liabilities ($218 million), much of this is tied up in a swelling inventories account. This, combined with more than $150 million in costs related to the corporate relocation to Tennessee has created the need for them to draw on their credit line to satisfy the cash requirements of their business.

Firearms sales exploded during the 2021-2023 COVID boom, pulling forward purchases in a range of industries including firearms. Manufacturers struggled with mangled supply chains and strong demand throughout this period, creating a supply/demand imbalance that paired increasing prices with depleted inventories. Just as manufacturers were getting caught back up, demand dropped as gun buyers had satiated their appetite for new firearms. This created another supply/demand imbalance (heavy on supply this time) which is what these companies are dealing with today.

Still, both companies have strong balance sheets which gives them the ability to repurchase shares at fairly attractive valuations today.

Returns on Capital

Low debt and strong cash flow are great traits in a business, but withholding leverage can depress returns, and it’s important that management reinvest excess cash wisely. Let’s look at these companies' recent track record on this front.

Measure

Company

6

28

10

Recent profitability metrics are very similar for this pair of companies. Smith & Wesson capitalized well on COVID-era demand in 2020 and 2021, while Ruger appears to have done somewhat better in the leaner period that has followed (and continues today). Ruger appears to have a stronger record on return on capital employed (ROCE) over the past decade, but Smith & Wesson’s returns may have been impacted by American Outdoor Brands prior to its 2020 divestiture. Also complicating a direct comparison is Smith & Wesson’s expensive relocation over the past 2 years. If either company has a clear advantage, it appears to be Smith & Wesson in gross and operating margins, which compare favorably to Ruger over most of the recent past. But this advantage disappears beyond the very recent history displayed on the chart above and neither of these measures take interest expense into account, which favors the debt-free Ruger.

Though neither firm stands apart on profitability metrics and returns have been “lumpy” (as opposed to the smooth line-up and to the right that Wall Street prefers), returns on capital have on average been quite good for both companies over the past decade.

Valuations

To this point, I’ve established that both Ruger and Smith & Wesson have strong balance sheets and generate adequate returns on capital. In this section, I’ll attempt to establish a conservative estimate of earnings power which may be used as a basis for valuation. The chart below spells out my estimate of mid-cycle earnings power for each company and is largely based on historical observations.

Measure (all numbers in $ millions except per-share figures)

Ruger

Smith & Wesson

Revenue

$520

$520

Gross Income

$140 (27% gross margin)

$172 (33% gross margin)

Operating Income

$68 (13% operating margin)

$52 (10% operating margin)

Net Income

$54

$39

EPS

$3.18

$0.85

Capital expenditures and DD&A are roughly aligned for both companies in most years, so simple earnings-per-share (rather than Buffett’s Owner Earnings) are a pretty good measure of the value being created for shareholders on an annual basis. With an estimate of EPS in-hand, I’ll need to put some thought into future growth rates to determine an earnings multiple that I’m comfortable paying.

Revenue growth has been fairly lackluster for both firms over the past decade. As mentioned above, sales in the firearms industry can be lumpy - characterized by surges brought on by societal worry swinging towards pullbacks as Americans decide they’re packing enough heat. Revenue for both firms shot up in the wake of Barack Obama’s election in 2008 as gun owners feared stricter gun control laws with a democratic administration. Sales dropped again when Trump was elected in 2016 before spiking in 2020 as COVID and societal unrest boosted demand.

Looking forward over the next few years, there isn’t any obvious catalyst (e.g. election) that’s likely to drive sales up again. That doesn’t mean there won't be (nobody saw COVID coming), but it’s wise to factor low near-term growth into any estimate of valuation. That also doesn’t mean these companies can’t drive sales growth through their own inventiveness. New products represented a substantial 27.6% of Smith & Wesson's net sales for the 12 months ended April 30, 2024 according to the company’s most recent 10-K. Ruger recently reported that 31.2% of firearms sales for the first 9 months of 2024 were from products introduced in the past two years. That shows that these companies do have levers to pull to generate demand, but it may come in the form of increased market share as opposed to higher overall industry sales.

Certainly, there will be unforeseen stimulants ahead for long-term investors over the upcoming decade. But this seems like the type of low-growth situation where shares must be acquired at a significant discount for investors looking to generate high returns on investment. I’m not banking on compound annual sales growth of more than 4% in the decade ahead, followed but 3% growth thereafter. Under that assumption, and with a goal of earning 15% on my capital, I’d be willing to pay a multiple between 8-9 on the earnings of either of these companies. That corresponds with a purchase price range of $25-$29/share for Ruger and $6.80-$7.65 for Smith & Wesson based on my estimates of earnings power above. Unfortunately, each company would have to decline by over 20% from today's already depressed levels to reach my target purchase price range. Before adding these companies to my watch list and waiting for them to reach my target purchase price, I’ll first evaluate their management and the risks involved.

Management

As I evaluate management for these two firms, I’ll be focusing on specific actions each company's leadership has taken that demonstrate sound judgment. I'll start with a tangible example of competent management from Ruger that inspires admiration.

In 2020, the Remington Outdoor Company filed for bankruptcy - a stark reminder of why Ruger and Smith & Wesson are wise to carry little to no debt. The company was broken up and sold for parts, with Ruger acquiring Marlin Firearms for $30 million. Marlin makes iconic lever-action rifles and has a history going back over 150 years. The purchase price was relatively modest for Ruger (payable with cash on hand), and they quickly started designing and planning production of a bevy of new rifles under the Marlin brand. In the 4 years since then, Ruger has released dozens of new models to critical acclaim, with long-time fans of the brand very happy with the products that Ruger is releasing. Marlin devotees have expressed pleasure with Ruger's efforts to keep the brand name alive and continue improvement of the product.

Ruger’s management did a great job recognizing the value of a brand tied up in its parent company’s bankruptcy and pouncing on the opportunity at an attractive valuation. Though Ruger doesn’t break out specific sales figures for Marlin, there are a number of enthusiastic articles online singing the company’s praises on the rebirth of a beloved brand. The Company’s CEO, Christopher Killoy, is a Westpoint graduate with more than 2 decades of experience as an executive in the firearms industry. That’s an impressive resume, and it’s backed up by demonstrated competence with Ruger’s debt-free balance sheet, solid returns on capital, and the tangible example of a successful investment in Marlin.

Turning to Smith & Wesson, although the company has been fairly active in the years since CEO Mark Smith took over (2020), it’s difficult to tease out any specific conclusions on management performance. Smith initiated the relocation to Tennessee, which has reduced the number of manufacturing and assembly facilities from four to three. But it’s too early to determine the financial impact of this decision as it has happened during a particularly volatile period. The relocation was expected to cost about $120 million but has ended up costing closer to $160 million, a possible sign of mismanagement. I’m more inclined to attribute this to the skyrocketing construction inflation that occurred soon after the initial announcement was made. One mark against Smith comes in the form of Smith & Wesson’s need to take on debt to manage rising inventories over the past 2 years. Again, the relocation hasn’t helped the company’s cash position during this period, but Smith lacks the clean sheet in this regard that Killoy possesses at Ruger.

Risks

Both Ruger and Smith & Wesson have fairly low financial risk as a result of low debt levels and strong cash generation. But they do face a certain amount of legal risk. Ruger and Smith & Wesson are currently defendants in a number of product liability lawsuits involving the criminal misuse of their product. Gunmakers are protected to some degree by the Protection of Lawful Commerce in Arms Act (PLCAA), which was signed into law in 2005 and shields firearm manufacturers, dealers and importers from civil lawsuits when their products are used to commit crimes. However, this legislation will be tested this year as the Supreme Court decides on Estados Unidos Mexicanos v. Smith & Wesson Brands, Inc., et al. This suit was filed by the Mexican government against a handful of American gunmakers (including Ruger and Smith & Wesson) and essentially alleges that these companies produce and market firearms in a way they know results in illegal trafficking of firearms into Mexico. Gun manufacturers actually petitioned the Supreme Court to take this case because any ruling that weakened the PLCAA could expose them to a significant amount of litigation. Given the protection offered by the PLCAA and the Supreme Court’s conservative tilt, it’s unlikely this case succeeds. However, political winds swing from right to left over time and may eventually expose manufacturers to significant liabilities under the wrong circumstances.

Summary

Both Ruger and Smith & Wesson have strong brands that are popular with gun owners across the country. Additionally, both companies have shown an ability to drive sales through the development of new products as evidenced by the percentage of sales made up by new products. However, if you held a gun to my head (ha) and made me pick one of these companies for the purpose of long-term investment, I’d have to lean towards Ruger. The differences are subtle, but I’ve come away with a bit more confidence in Ruger’s management compared with Smith & Wesson's. Specifically, I find their management of the current industry pullback without taking on any debt and the successful rejuvenation of a brand acquired cheaply out of bankruptcy (Marlin) to be very impressive. Unfortunately, I don’t think either of these firms are priced at a level that yields a good chance at fantastic future returns given the fact that both are likely to be low-growth companies going forward. However, I’ve put both on my personal watchlist and will be watching for insider purchases and a pullback in price going forward. If the price becomes compelling and insiders start making significant purchases, I'll be an enthusiastic buyer.

data.title: Cobalt Miners News For The Month Of February 2025
data.description:
Welcome to the February 2025 cobalt miners news.

The past month saw cobalt prices significantly lower, hitting a multi-decade low.

Cobalt Price News

As of February 19, the cobalt spot price was at US$9.77/lb, lower from US$11.02/lb last month. The LME cobalt price is US$20,905/tonne. LME Cobalt inventory is 126 tonnes, flat from 126 tonnes from last month. More details on cobalt pricing (in particular the more relevant cobalt sulphate), can be found here at Benchmark Mineral Intelligence or Fast Markets MB.

Cobalt spot prices long-term price chart - USD 9.77 (Source)

Cobalt Demand Vs. Supply Forecasts

S&P Global Market intelligence - Cobalt demand vs. supply forecast as of Oct. 2024 (Source)

The global cobalt market balance is forecast to shift from surplus to deficit in 2027 (as of June 2024) (Source)

Morgan Stanley forecasts cobalt surpluses to grow this decade (as of Nov. 2023) (Source)

Trend Investing vs. IEA 2021 demand forecast for EV metals

2021 IEA forecast growth in demand for selected minerals from clean energy technologies by scenario, 2040 relative to 2020 - Increases Of Lithium 13x to 42x, Graphite 8x to 25x, Cobalt 6x to 21x, Nickel 7x to 19x, Manganese 3x to 8x, Rare Earths 3x to 7x, And Copper 2x to 3x

Cobalt Market News

On January 30, Miningdotcom reported:

Cobalt price plunge and the EV market...In a recent note, BMO Capital Markets pointed out that in fact real cobalt prices are at the lowest in history based on annual average prices adjusted for inflation going back to the late 1920s using USGS data. Nearly all cobalt output is a byproduct of nickel and copper mining. Copper production in the Congo is rising fast leading to a near 40% jump in the country's cobalt output in 2024. The DRC already accounts for 80% of global cobalt supply, estimated to have exceeded 300,000 tonnes in 2024.

On February 6, Miningdotcom reported:

Column: Another cobalt bust but this time it's different...But while it was an artisanal mining surge that caused the bust of 2018-2019, this time around it's China's giant CMOC Group. The company more than doubled its output of cobalt last year, pumping nearly 60,000 tons of extra metal into a global market of just over 200,000 tons.

On February 14, Fastmarkets reported:

Cobalt market faces oversupply hurdles amid China's demand watch and price challenges...CMOC's guidance for 2025 set at 100-120kt of cobalt suggests we're in for another bearish year for the market. In the US, the potential tariffs on Canadian cobalt metal could see short-term tightening on certain Western brands, however, we're not holding out for a strong recovery in standard-grade cobalt metal pricing for the rest of the year.

Cobalt Company News

CMOC Group Limited [HKSE:3993] [SHE:603993] (OTCPK:CMCLF) (formerly China Molybdenum)

No significant news for the month.

Glencore [HK:805] [LSE:GLEN] (OTCPK:GLCNF)

On January 30, Glencore announced: "Full Year 2024 Production Report." 2024 own sourced cobalt production was 38.2kt down 8% YoY.

On February 19, Glencore announced:

2025 Share buy-back programme. Glencore plc (the "Company") announces the commencement of a programme to make market purchases of its ordinary shares (the "Shares") of an aggregate value of up to USD 1 billion (the "Programme"), subject to market conditions, with completion planned to be by 6 August 2025.

On February 19, Glencore announced:

Preliminary Results 2024..."Our strong operational performance, along with another strong Marketing contribution, supported the generation of Adjusted EBITDA of $14.4 billion and Funds from operations of $10.5 billion during 2024, down 16%, but up 11%, respectively compared to 2023. The decline in Adjusted EBITDA, particularly within the Industrial segment, was mainly a function of lower average energy coal prices year over year...We are pleased to announce c.$2.2 billion (c.$0.182 per share) of shareholder returns in accordance with our capital allocation framework. We are recommending a $0.10 per share (c.$1.2 billion) base cash distribution, together with a 'top-up' buyback of $1.0 billion (c.$0.082 per share)...Net income attributable to equity holders pre-significant items: $3.7 billion; Net loss attributable to equity holders: $1.6 billion.

On February 19, Reuters reported:

Glencore looks at leaving London as shares drop and earnings dip... "Weaker coal prices have been a major headwind for Glencore over the past six months"...The company's net debt rose to $11.2 billion, from $4.9 billion in 2023, partly due to $6.7 billion of net capex and the $7 billion acquisition of metallurgical coal portfolio EVR, which it bought from Teck Resources. This was above analysts' consensus expectation for debt of $8.7 billion.

Zheijiang Huayou Cobalt [SHA:603799]

On February 13, Market Screener reported: "Huayou Cobalt's controlling shareholder to raise company holdings."

Jinchuan Group International Resources [HK:2362]

No news for the month.

Chemaf (subsidiary of Shalina Resources) - deal to be taken over by Norin Mining Limited in Q4, 2024, but Congo wants this halted. More recent news here.

No news for the month.

GEM Co Ltd [SHE:002340]

No significant news for the month. The company did report on their grand 2025 Opening Ceremony.

Eurasian Resources Group ("ERG") - private

ERG owns the Metalkol facility in the DRC where ERG processes cobalt and copper tailings with a capacity of up to 24,000 tonnes of cobalt pa.

On January 27, ERG announced: "Eurasian Resources Group sees hefty growth in copper, stainless steel and aluminium markets in 2025." No mention of cobalt.

Umicore SA [Brussels:UMI] (OTCPK:UMICY)

On February 14, Umicore announced: "Full Year Results 2024. Continued robust performance of foundation businesses; Battery Materials: actions taken to counteract headwinds.

  • Revenues1 of € 3.5 billion
  • Adjusted EBITDA of € 763 million and adjusted EBITDA margin of 22.0%
  • Adjustments to EBITDA of -€ 1.79 billion (mainly because of a -€ 1.60 billion non-cash impairment and write down in Battery Materials in the first half of 2024)
  • Adjusted EBIT of € 478 million
  • Adjusted net profit (Group share) of € 255 million and adjusted EPS of € 1.06...
  • More than € 100 million in EBITDA from efficiency measures, ahead of the € 70 million target...
  • Proposed gross annual dividend for 2024 of € 0.50 per share of which € 0.25 to be paid in April 2025, with an interim dividend of € 0.25 already paid out in August 2024...."

"In light of slower-than-expected growth in demand for electric vehicles and declining metal prices, Umicore has been taking steps to reassess its growth projections and to further adjust its capital expenditures as well as its cost base to the new market reality. This includes the launch of a strategic review and a realignment of the workforce as part of the overall cost-saving strategy."

Sumitomo Metal Mining Co. (TYO:5713) (OTCPK:STMNF)

On February 12, Market Screener reported:

Cobalt Blue Holdings [ASX:COB] (OTCPK:CBBHF) - Plan to change name to Core Blue Minerals Limited

Cobalt Blue has 87kt of contained cobalt at their 100% owned Broken Hill Cobalt Project [BHCP] in NSW, Australia. There is also a plan for a Cobalt-Nickel Refinery. LG International is an equity strategic partner. COB also has an earn-in agreement (earn in to 51% up to 75%) for the Halls Creek Project in WA (copper-lead-zinc-silver-gold).

On February 18, Cobalt Blue announced:

COB diversifies - Earn-in agreement for major copper project. Cobalt Blue has entered into an earn-in agreement with AuKing Mining (ASX:AKN) for the Halls Creek Project, a large-scale copper-lead-zinc-silver-gold asset with significant cobalt potential. The Halls Creek Project is favourably located within the Kimberley region of Western Australia; just 15km southwest of Halls Creek and 320km south of Wyndham port. The area has historically supported a number of gold, nickel and cobalt operations. The Project hosts two major deposits with existing Mineral Resources containing a combined 89kt copper, 69kt lead, 326kt zinc, 9.2Moz silver and 45koz gold....The proposed new name, Core Blue Minerals Limited, aligns with COB's evolution beyond cobalt into a diversified commodity portfolio.

Australian Mines [ASX:AUZ] (OTCPK:AMSLF)

On January 29, Australian Mines announced:

Quarterly Activities/Appendix 5B Cash Flow Report. Strong scandium demand growth and increased interest in this metal prompted AUZ to review the historical Flemington scoping study resulting in a significant increase in the Mineral Resources (JORC 21012) at Flemington. During the period the Company continued its diversification into Brazil, initiating its maiden drilling programme carried out at Jequie North and Jequie South. The company is awaiting drill assay results from this programme which are to be released to the market once received. While the nickel and cobalt markets continue to be under pressure, the Company continues to hold the Sconi project in good standing while seeking opportunities to create shareholder value.

Conclusion

February saw cobalt spot prices significantly lower and LME inventory levels flat.

Highlights for the month were:

  • BMO Capital Markets: Real cobalt prices are at the lowest in history based on annual average prices adjusted for inflation back to the late 1920s.
  • Fastmarkets: CMOC's guidance for 2025 set at 100-120kt of cobalt suggests we're in for another bearish year for the market.
  • Glencore 2024 own sourced cobalt production was 38.2kt, down 8% YoY. Announced 2025 up to US$1B share buyback. Weaker coal prices have been a major headwind for Glencore over the past six months.
  • Umicore - Battery Materials: actions taken to counteract headwinds. Launch of a strategic review and a realignment of the workforce as part of the overall cost-saving strategy.
  • Ramu Nickel-Cobalt Mine returns to full production capacity.
  • Jervois Global commences Chapter 11 recapitalisation procedure.
  • Electra - Gold-rich cobalt-copper mineralization at CAS Property strengthens Electra's Idaho exploration strategy.
  • Sunrise Energy Metals - Sunrise Project continued with activities focused on completing studies for the long-lead electrical transmission line etc.
  • Ardea Resources - Significant progress has been made in advancing the KNP - Goongarrie Hub DFS.
  • Cobalt Blue - COB diversifies - Earn-in agreement for major copper project (Halls Creek Project) in WA. Proposes name change to Core Blue Minerals Limited.
  • TMC - World First: TMC and PAMCO achieve a new nodule processing milestone.

As usual, all comments are welcome.
